retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus is most common in infancy secondary to congenital malformations and from intraventricular hemorrhage in premature babies. Late in life, it presents another peak due to NPH cases. The overall global prevalence of hydrocephalus is approximately 85 per 100,000 individuals with a significant difference between different age groups; 88 per 100,000 for the pediatric population and 11 per 100,000 in adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal hemorrhage or infection can cause hydrocephalus. Some genetic forms of hydrocephalus may not be evident at birth. Almost 10% of all cases of hydrocephalus in newborns is due to brainstem malformation with stenosis of the cerebral aqueduct. Dandy-Walker malformation will be the cause in 2-4% of hydrocephalus in this age group. Arnold-Chiari type 1 and type 2, agenesis of the foramen of Monro, Bickers-Adams syndrome are common syndromes in newborns with hydrocephalus. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation and management of hydrocephalus require a multidisciplinary approach; many specialties are involved in the care of such patients. Neurologists Neurosurgeons Neurorehabilitation specialists Ophthalmologists Pediatrician

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital hydrocephalus is usually present at birth. An unusually large head is a significant sign of congenital hydrocephalus. There are many more symptoms associated with congenital hydrocephalus like tense and bulge fontanelle, a disjunction of sutures, thin and shiny scalp with prominently visible veins, stiff arms, and legs prone to contractions, “the setting sun” outlook (pupils of the eyes may be close to the lower eyelid), breathing difficulties, poor feeding, the unwillingness of the infant to bend or move their neck or head, delayed developmental stages. A “cracked pot” sound on percussion of the skull is the so-called Macewen sign.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Some institutions have an estimated mortality rate of 12% to 50%. [1] Treatment of hydrocephalus with shunting substantially improves mortality. [12] Fetal mortality directly correlates with the presence of extra-CNS abnormalities. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis largely depends on the cause of hydrocephalus. Half of the patients with severe intraventricular hemorrhage may require definite treatment. In children, after posterior fossa surgery, around 20% will require permanent shunting.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hydrocephalus is a medical emergency. For those cases where a shunt can not be placed urgently, an anterior fontanelle ventricular tap may be performed in infants. In adults and children, placement of external ventricular drain is the most common procedure performed in these cases. Lumbar puncture in posthemorrhagic and postmeningitic hydrocephalus is an option.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of hydrocephalus. Describe the appropriate evaluation of hydrocephalus. Outline the management options available for hydrocephalus. Discuss interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance health care and improve outcomes for patients with hydrocephalus. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydranencephaly -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (severe-extreme): This can occur due to multiple etiologies. The cortical mantle is preserved. There is the presence of the third ventricle, abnormal head circumference at birth, full and bulging fontanelles, and a normal vascular study. There is significant clinical and radiographic improvement after shunt placement, which can improve prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Myelomeningocele -- Treatment / Management -- Hydrocephalus Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Despite advances in reducing the need for shunt placement through prenatal surgery, approximately 80% of children undergoing postnatal closure still require shunt placement. [35] Treatment for hydrocephalus in this population may involve shunting or endoscopic third ventriculostomy, with or without choroid plexus cauterization. Treatment criteria typically include an increase in the greatest occipitofrontal circumference crossing percentiles, worsening hydrocephalus on follow-up imaging, a head circumference above the 95th percentile, and physical examination findings such as a bulging fontanelle, sunsetting sign, or separation of cranial sutures.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence in the elderly population is much higher, somewhere around 175 per 100,000, and more than 400 per 100,000 for those more than 80 years old due to the high incidence of NPH later in life. Africa and South America have higher hydrocephalus prevalence. [7] Infantile hydrocephalus prevalence is between 1 and 32 per 10,000 births. [8] Both genders are generally equally affected.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: To determine if the macrocephaly is related to another process or syndrome, a careful history should be taken with attention to birth measurements and growth trajectory, any other associated medical problems, a developmental history, and any family history of macrocephaly. For instance, it would be important to know if the patient has had any history of CNS trauma or infection. If there were meningitis or intraventricular hemorrhage in the neonatal state, hydrocephalus could develop as a result, and these patients should be followed closely. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus is the symptomatic accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) inside the cerebral ventricles. [1] This accumulation may be due to obstruction in the normal flow of the CSF, or to problems with absorption into the venous system by the Pacchionian arachnoid granulations, or due to excessive production of CSF. Dandy first describes hydrocephalus as communicating and non-communicating (obstructive) in early 1913, and since then, many more classifications were proposed. In adults, there are four different types; obstructive, communicating, hypersecretory, and normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH). Congenital or developmental hydrocephalus is often present at birth and is often part of a genetic syndrome or spinal dysraphism.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Fifty percent of children with untreated hydrocephalus die before age 3, and only approximately 20% to 23% will reach adult life. Most of the patients with untreated hydrocephalus who reach adult life will have motor, visual, and auditory deficits. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocephalus -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A multidisciplinary team approach is crucial in the treatment of hydrocephalus, especially in children. Their health care needs are extended beyond childhood and adolescence. Health care transition is a process of leaving the pediatric and entering the adult health care system, but many families and patients face this transition period unprepared. Best practices are identified, and consensus recommendations are provided to facilitate this challenging period. The multidisciplinary team involving a pediatrician, neurosurgeon, ophthalmologist, and rehabilitation specialist offers the patient with integrated care for a good outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Dandy-Walker Malformation -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation is nonspecific, subject to multiple factors, including the severity of hydrocephalus, intracranial hypertension, and associated comorbidities. [1] Most patients will present in their first year of life with signs and symptoms of increased intracranial pressure. [6] [7] [12] The most common manifestation is macrocephaly, affecting 90% to 100% of patients during their first months of life. [7] The syndromic form of DWM may also have malformations of the heart, face, limbs, and gastrointestinal or genitourinary system that could draw initial medical attention. [9] Almost 75% of cases will have hydrocephalus by the age of 3 months, and eventually, almost 90% of the patients will have hydrocephalus. A case series of 42 patients with DWM showed hydrocephaly in all patients at the time of diagnosis, vermian hypoplasia in 88%, and cerebellar hypoplasia in 59%. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2149
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a prenatal growth abnormality that manifests during the fetal period. In the same developmental window, other fluid‑related fetal conditions also arise, including wanverhouding with foetale ascites, wanverhouding with foetale hydrops, and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal cerebral hydrocephalus causing disproportion). These entities are all linked by their occurrence in the fetal period, reflecting a spectrum of fetal fluid accumulation and structural imbalance that can be identified through prenatal imaging and require coordinated obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: or other delivery complications. Recognizing how each of these specific conditions—both maternal (e.g., tumor of cervix, multiple gestation) and fetal (e.g., myelomeningocele, hydrops, ascites, hydrocephalus, malposition)—fit within the larger framework of MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY, and how they are tied to the fetal period, helps clinicians anticipate potential labor difficulties, plan appropriate interventions, and improve overall maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: term malposition/malpresentation within obstetric terminology. These findings reflect عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين (instability of fetal position) and are used in clinical guidelines for “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” to guide recognition, classification, and management, which may include specialized labor management, operative delivery, cesarean section, or intensified monitoring to protect maternal and neonatal safety. Cephalopelvic disproportion (عدم تناسب الرأس والحوض) is a child concept of Fetal Positions and Presentations and is further connected to structural fetal anomalies such as fetal myelomeningocele and fetal brain hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) that cause the mismatch. The fetal period is also associated with pathological states including foetale ascites (wanverhouding; foetale ascites), foetale hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops), and other fetal period pathologies that can contribute to abnormal presentations. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2205
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a condition that manifests during the fetal period. The fetal period itself is linked to other prenatal abnormalities, including wanverhouding; foetale ascites and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب (fetal hydrocephalus causing disproportion). Recognizing that fetal disproportion associated with myelomeningocele, as well as ascites and hydrocephalus, all occur within the same developmental window underscores the importance of comprehensive fetal assessment to identify and manage multiple concurrent anomalies.

The main category is also connected to normal obstetric processes through the relationships “Normal delivery procedure” and “Normal pregnancy &/or delivery (spontaneous vaginal delivery).” This indicates that, in the absence of the aforementioned maternal or fetal abnormalities, labor typically proceeds via standard physiological mechanisms leading to a spontaneous vaginal birth. Overall, the network of entities underscores that both maternal cervical pathology and a range of fetal abnormalities—including malposition, myelomeningocele‑related disproportion, ascites, hydrops, and hydrocephalus—are integral to understanding and managing labor and delivery. Recognizing these interrelated conditions enables clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 962
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery comprise a broad group of specific obstetric complications that arise during the fetal period. Within this group, a breech malpresentation that is successfully converted to a cephalic presentation represents a correctable fetal malposition, while other fetal malpositions such as “سوء تموضع الجنين” (fetal malposition) remain important considerations for delivery planning. Fetal disproportion caused by fetal myelomeningocele is another condition that occurs in the fetal period and can lead to delivery difficulties. Additional fetal abnormalities that belong to this category include fetal ascites (“wanverhouding; foetale ascites”), fetal hydrops (“wanverhouding; foetale hydrops”), and fetal brain hydrocephalus (“استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب”), each of which can produce disproportionate growth and complicate labor. All of these entities—breech malpresentation conversion, fetal myelomeningocele‑related disproportion, fetal malposition, fetal ascites, fetal hydrops, and fetal brain hydrocephalus—are classified as child concepts of the overarching category “MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY” and are manifested during the fetal period, highlighting their relevance for prenatal assessment and intrapartum management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: are linked as children of the overarching category, indicating that each represents a distinct maternal or fetal factor that may affect labor and delivery outcomes. The category MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is also associated with normal obstetric processes, specifically Normal delivery procedure and Normal pregnancy &/or delivery (spontaneous vaginal delivery). This relationship underscores that the identified conditions are considered in the context of both routine and complicated deliveries, guiding clinicians in assessing whether standard delivery pathways are appropriate or whether special management is required. The Fetal period is identified as the temporal context in which several fetal conditions occur. The Fetal period occurs in wanverhouding; foetaal, wanverhouding; foetale ascites, wanverhouding; foetale hydrops, and استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب, indicating that these fluid‑related and hydrocephalus‑related fetal abnormalities arise

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 961
retrieved_docs.content: presentation” illustrates a scenario in which an initially unfavorable fetal position is corrected, improving the likelihood of a vaginal birth. Conversely, “سوء تموضع الجنين” (fetal malposition) and “Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele” represent fetal factors that can create mechanical mismatch between the fetus and the maternal pelvis, potentially leading to obstructed labor. The latter condition is specifically linked to the “Fetal period,” indicating that the disproportion arises during fetal development. Additional fetal pathologies listed as children of the main category include “wanverhouding; foetaal,” “wanverhouding; foetale ascites,” “wanverhouding; foetale hydrops,” and “استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب” (fetal brain hydrocephalus causing disproportion). These conditions—fetal ascites, hydrops fetalis, and hydrocephalus—can increase fetal size or alter fetal shape, further contributing to labor complications. The main category is also connected to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2336
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele is a condition that manifests during the fetal period. The fetal period itself is the developmental stage in which a range of related abnormalities can also be observed, including wanverhouding; foetaal, wanverhouding; foetale ascites, and wanverhouding; foetale hydrops. In addition, fetal hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) can arise in the same period and may further contribute to fetal disproportion. Together, these entities highlight that multiple fetal‑specific pathologies—myelomeningocele‑related disproportion, ascites, hydrops, and hydrocephalus—share the common temporal context of the fetal period, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and monitoring of fetal growth and fluid balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 651
retrieved_docs.content: MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY is a broad category that includes a variety of specific maternal and fetal abnormalities known to influence the course of labor and delivery. Among the fetal conditions listed are breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation, fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele, and fetal brain hydrocephalus (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب) that also leads to disproportion. Additional fetal complications captured under this category are foetal malposition (wanverhouding; foetaal), foetal ascites (wanverhouding; foetale ascites), and foetal hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops). The category also encompasses multiple gestations such as septuplet pregnancy, quadruplet pregnancy (حمل بأربعة توائم), and twin pregnancy (حمل توأمي). All of these specific conditions are considered “children” of the overarching category, indicating that they are subtypes of maternal or fetal factors that can affect delivery outcomes. Moreover, fetal disproportion due to fetal myelomeningocele, as well as the conditions of foetal malposition, foetal ascites, foetal hydrops, and fetal brain hydrocephalus, are all associated with the fetal period, highlighting that they arise during fetal development. Recognizing how these diverse conditions interrelate within the context of the fetal period helps clinicians anticipate potential delivery complications, tailor obstetric management, and improve both maternal and neonatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site” includes the specific procedure referred to in Arabic as “التحويلة البطينية البريتونية” (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). This type of shunt diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the brain’s ventricles to an extracranial location, typically the peritoneal cavity, and is employed in the management of conditions such as hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 834
retrieved_docs.content: event that can affect fetal well‑being and labor progress. Overall, the network of relationships shows that MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY integrates fetal developmental disorders (such as myelomeningocele, hydrocephalus, and fluid accumulations), maternal pelvic dynamics (pelvic inlet, outlet, and overall contraction), and standard delivery practices (normal delivery procedure and spontaneous vaginal delivery). Recognizing how these entities interrelate is essential for clinicians to assess risk, plan appropriate interventions, and optimize outcomes for both mother and fetus during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1567
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt is classified as a ventriculoatrial shunt procedure, reflecting its function of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to the atrium for the management of hydrocephalus and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 870
retrieved_docs.content: are all noted to occur in the fetal period. The interrelationship among these entities underscores that a range of maternal conditions (such as cervical polyps) and fetal conditions (including myelomeningocele‑related disproportion, ascites, hydrops fetalis, and hydrocephalus) can directly affect labor and delivery outcomes. Recognizing that these specific complications belong to the overarching category of maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery, and that the fetal abnormalities develop during the fetal period, is essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version for breech or surgical management of cervical polyps), and optimize both maternal and neonatal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: fetal hydrops (wanverhouding; foetale hydrops), and سوء تموضع الجنين. Pelvic contractile dynamics—pelvic contraction (تقلص الحوض), pelvic inlet contraction (تقلص مدخل الحوض), and pelvic outlet contraction (تقلص مخرج الحوض)—constitute additional obstetric factors, together with fetal brain hydrocephalus causing disproportion (استسقاء دماغي للجنين مسبب لعدم التناسب). Normal delivery procedures and spontaneous vaginal delivery are linked concepts, indicating that many of these conditions are managed within routine vaginal birth when feasible. All of these maternal and fetal events occur during the fetal period, a developmental stage in which clinicians must anticipate potential labor complications and plan appropriate intrapartum interventions.
